content.full_text:
July 5, 1994

William J. Dawson
President, Stones/Bengard Community
Services District

Betty Beddingfield
Secretary, Stones/Bengard Community
Services District

509-695 Stone Road
Susanville, CA 96130

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-180

Dear Mr. Dawson and Ms. Beddingfield:

You have requested advice on behalf of William J. Dawson,
Delbert Hewett, William Sievers, and Kenneth Wheeler regarding
their duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

This letter pertains to telephone advice I provided you on
June 9, 1994, concerning the "public generally" exception under
the Act. Please note that this letter is based on the facts
presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May William J. Dawson, Delbert Hewett, William Sievers, and
Kenneth Wheeler, Directors of the Stones/Bengard Community
Services District and members of a sewer system cooperative by
virtue of their real property interests, participate in district
decisions concerning the purchase of the Cooperative?


¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000 - 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-180
Page 2


CONCLUSION

According to your facts, the real property of Directors
Delbert Hewett and Kenneth Wheeler will not be affected by the
district decisions. Therefore, they may participate in the
decisions concerning the purchase of the Cooperative. Moreover,
it appears that Directors William J. Dawson and William Sievers
will be affected in a substantially similar manner as a
significant segment of property owners in the community services
district. Thus, they are permitted to participate in the
decisions pursuant to the "public generally" exception of the Act.

FACTS

The Stones/Bengard Community Services District (the "CSD") is
a public agency which is in the process of forming an assessment
district to buy-out the Stones Bengard Sanitary Cooperative (the
"Cooperative"). William J. Dawson, Delbert Hewett, William
Sievers, and Kenneth Wheeler are directors of the CSD.

The Cooperative is a nonprofit organization which installed
the sewer system for the entire area of the Stones Bengard
Subdivision located on the north end of Eagle Lake in Lassen
County. The Cooperative was created with state and county
assistance and was provided an interim permit to provide sewer
services in the area until the newly formed CSD became financially
able to take over sewer services.

Of the five directors on the CSD, these four public officials
are also members of the Cooperative. However, they do not receive
any income from the Cooperative. These four officials purchased
shares in the Cooperative in order to connect their real property
into the Cooperative's sewer system. Directors William J. Dawson
and William Sievers each own two parcels which were connected to
the Cooperative's sewer system and Directors Delbert Hewett and
Kenneth Wheeler each own one parcel which was connected.

Within the CSD, there are approximately 297 parcel owners.
There are 168 parcel owners within the CSD who are members of the
Cooperative. Except for a few parcel owners, the property of the
parcel owners within the district consists of residential lots
which are approximately one to two acres.

It is expected that upon assumption by the CSD of the
Cooperative functions, each of the property owners who own a
parcel or parcels which have not been connected to sewer services
by virtue of membership in the Cooperative will be assessed a fee
for sewer services per parcel. Existing members of the
Cooperative will not have to pay an additional fee for the parcels
of land which have been connected to the Cooperative's sewer
system. William J. Dawson and William Sievers each own an
additional parcel of land which will be assessed a fee for sewer
services if the CSD assumes the Cooperative's functions.

Our File No. A-94-180
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family, or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Section 87103(b).

As members of the CSD, the CSD directors are public
officials. (Section 82048; Lyon Advice Letter, No. 88-391.)
Accordingly, they may not make, participate in making, or attempt
to use their official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on their real property interests.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal. App. 3d 983,
989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817, 822; In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow,
supra at 823.)

Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is
"materially" affected as required by Section 87103. Commission
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth standards to determine when a
decision will have a material financial effect on real property
interests which are the subject of a decision. (Sloan Advice
Letter, No. A-93-095.)

In the present situation, the CSD's decisions involve the
purchase of the Cooperative in order to form a sewer assessment
district. Thus, all of the officials have real property which
will in some way be affected by the decisions.

Our File No. A-94-180
Page 4


Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) provides, in pertinent part, that a
governmental decision will have a material financial effect on an
official's real property interest where:

(A) The decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale,
purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion
from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of real property in which
the official has a direct or indirect interest
(other than a leasehold interest) of $1,000 or
more, or a similar decision affecting such
property;

* * *

(C) The decision involves the imposition,
repeal or modification of any taxes or fees
assessed or imposed on such property; or

(Emphasis added.)

Since the decisions will result in the inclusion of the
directors' property in an assessment district and/or in the
imposition of a fee to be assessed upon the directors' real
property, the effect on their property is deemed material pursuant
to Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).

Directors Delbert Hewett and Kenneth Wheeler each own one
parcel which will be included in the CSD as a result of the
decision. However, according to a telephone conversation with
William Dawson on June 24, 1994, since these officials' property
has already been connected to the Cooperative's sewer system, no
additional fee will be assessed by the CSD against their property.
Thus, it appears that these officials' real property will not be
affected by the decision and they may participate in the decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(c)(2).)

However, Directors Dawson and Sievers each own a parcel of
land which will be subject to the assessment fee. Consequently,
they may not participate in decisions concerning the purchase of
the Cooperative unless the effect on their financial interest is
the same as the effect on the public generally.

"Public Generally" Exception

For the "public generally" exception to apply, the decisions
must affect the directors' interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect a significant segment of the public in
the jurisdiction. (Section 87103 and Regulation 18703.)

Our File No. A-94-180
Page 5


Regulation 18703(a) provides:

     [T]he material financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's
economic interests is indistinguishable from its
effect on the public generally if both subdivisions
(a)(1) and (a)(2) of this regulation apply:

     (1) Significant Segment: The
governmental decision will affect a
"significant segment" of the public generally
as set forth below:

          (A) The decision will affect:

               (i) Ten percent or more of the
          population in the jurisdiction of
          the official's agency or the
          district the official represents, or

               (ii) Ten percent or more of
          all property owners, all home
          owners, or all households in the
          jurisdiction of the official's
          agency or the district the official
          represents, or

                              * * *

     (2) Substantially the Same Manner: The
governmental decision will affect the
official's economic interest in substantially
the same manner as it will affect the economic
interests of the segment identified in
subdivision (a)(1) of this regulation.

     The jurisdiction in question is the Stones/Bengard Community
Services District. (Section 82035.) According to your facts,
there are 297 parcel owners in the CSD and 168 parcel owners who
are members of the Cooperative.

     On the basis of your facts, the decision will affect the
property owners in the jurisdiction who own property which is not
part of the Cooperative. Directors Dawson and Sievers each own a
parcel which will be affected by the decision. Thus, if a CSD
decision will affect a significant segment of the public generally
pursuant to Regulation 18703(a)(1) they may participate in the
decision. Regulation 18703(a)(1)(A)(ii) provides that a
significant segment is affected by a governmental decision if it
will affect ten percent or more of all property owners, all home
owners, or all households in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency or the district the official represents.

Our File No. A-94-180
Page 6


On the basis of your facts, it appears that approximately 129
or 44 percent of the parcel owners within the CSD own property
which has not been connected to the Cooperative's sewer system.
Thus, it appears that a significant segment of the public will be
affected by the decisions concerning the purchase of the
Cooperative for purposes of Regulation 18703(a)(1)(A)(ii).

However, to participate, Directors Dawson and Sievers must be
affected in substantially the same manner as a significant segment
of the public. (Regulation 18703(a)(2).) According to your
facts, except for a few parcel owners, the property of the parcel
owners within the CSD consists of residential lots which are
approximately one to two acres, of which these public officials
each own one parcel. Thus, it appears that they would be affected
by the decisions concerning the purchase of the Cooperative in a
similar manner as a significant segment of the public, and they
may also participate in the decisions.

I trust that this answers your questions. If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
